sound_type: low musical honk flocks -- rolling throaty 'crronk' or 'r-r-rronk' at 200-1200 Hz, continuous flock murmur on eelgrass beds, more melodious than Canada Goose, softer than Cackling, E=4 flock-driven communal sound
ecosystem: Coastal bay, winter visitor. Feeds almost exclusively on eelgrass (Zostera marina) in shallow coastal bays. Breeds on Arctic tundra. Winters on protected saltwater bays and estuaries along the Pacific Coast.
season: Winter visitor (late October-May). Breeds on Arctic tundra in summer. Migration along Pacific Coast. Padilla Bay and Boundary Bay primary PNW sites.

coordinates.description: Reference for Padilla Bay, Skagit County, WA -- primary PNW Brant wintering site. 8,000 acres of eelgrass. Padilla Bay NERR. Thousands of Brant at low tide.

taxonomy_note.description: FOURTEENTH consecutive Anatidae (118-131). Record extends massively. E=4 reflects the flock-driven, musical quality of Brant vocalizations. Third goose in the goose era (129-134): Canada (129), Cackling (130), Brant (131). The eelgrass specialist -- most ecologically constrained goose in the PNW. Third Branta genus entry. Vulnerable to eelgrass decline.
taxonomy_note.current_family: Anatidae (14th consecutive -- RECORD EXTENDS MASSIVELY, streak ongoing)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Branta from Old Norse brandgas ('burnt goose'). bernicla from Latin bernacla (barnacle goose) -- originally thought to grow from barnacles in medieval folklore. The 'barnacle goose' name persists in some European traditions.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Branta -- third entry. Canada Goose (129), Cackling Goose (130), Brant (131). Three consecutive Branta species.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anseriformes. Fourteenth consecutive Anatidae. The goose era continues.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae at fourteenth consecutive. The streak now spans ducks (118-126), swans (127-128), and geese (129-131). The eelgrass specialist adds the marine dimension.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 200-1,200 Hz. Lower than Cackling Goose (400-2,000 Hz), overlapping with but distinct from Canada Goose (200-2,500 Hz). Narrower range, centered lower.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 300-800 Hz. Lower energy center contributes to the muffled, musical quality. The rolling 'r' character is distinctive.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Murmur: 200-1,200 Hz. Flight call: slightly higher, more nasal. Alarm: sharper, higher-pitched.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual calls 0.5-1 second. Continuous murmuring in feeding flocks. The flock murmur is the defining sound -- continuous, not discrete calls.
song_metrics.note_structure: LOW MUSICAL HONK: Rolling, throaty, gargling. 'Crronk' or 'r-r-rronk.' The rolled 'r' quality is distinctive. More melodious than Canada Goose, softer than Cackling Goose.
song_metrics.note_types: Flock murmur (primary, continuous feeding sound), flight call (slightly higher, nasal), alarm (sharper, triggered by Bald Eagles), greeting (between pair members).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Continuous murmuring in flocks. Hundreds of birds feeding produce a low-pitched conversational gabble that fills a bay. Not discrete honking (Canada Goose) or rapid cackling (Cackling Goose) but continuous rolling murmur.
song_metrics.timbre: Low. Rolling. Throaty. Musical. The gargling quality with rolled 'r' is unique among PNW geese. More liquid than Canada Goose. Softer than Cackling Goose.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Individual call is moderate. Flock murmur is immersive. Hundreds of Brant feeding on eelgrass produce a continuous low soundscape. Not acoustically dominant like Canada Goose (E=5) but more musical.
song_metrics.tempo: Continuous. The flock murmur has no discrete tempo -- it is a constant, rolling gabble. Individual calls within the murmur have moderate repetition rate.
song_metrics.context: Flock communication, feeding, flight, alarm, pair bonding. The flock murmur during feeding is the primary and most characteristic vocalization.
song_metrics.regional_variation: The Black Brant (B. b. nigricans, Pacific) and Atlantic Brant (B. b. hrota) have similar vocalizations. Minimal geographic variation within subspecies.
song_metrics.key_property: LOW MUSICAL HONK FLOCKS -- E=4 FLOCK-DRIVEN COMMUNAL SOUND. Rolling throaty 'crronk' at 200-1,200 Hz. Flock murmur on eelgrass beds. More melodious than Canada Goose. FOURTEENTH CONSECUTIVE ANATIDAE (118-131). Third Branta. Third goose in goose era. Eelgrass specialist -- most ecologically constrained PNW goose. Padilla Bay primary site (8,000 acres eelgrass). Black Brant subspecies. Arctic tundra breeder. Canary in the eelgrass mine. Indicator species for coastal bay health.

plumage.overall: Dark overall. Black head, neck, breast, and back. White necklace mark (incomplete, usually a patch). Dark belly with narrow white barring on flanks. Short black bill. Small size (1.0-1.8 kg).
plumage.diagnostic_mark: NO white cheek patch (unlike Canada/Cackling Geese). White necklace. All-dark appearance. Small size. On saltwater bays (not fields or parks).
plumage.seasonal_variation: Adults similar year-round. Juveniles slightly browner with less distinct necklace.
plumage.flight_pattern: Low over water in dense flocks. Rapid wingbeats. Dark and compact. Rolling flight chorus.

physical_description.overall: Body length 56-66 cm (22-26 inches). Weight 1.0-1.8 kg (2.2-4 lbs). Similar size to Cackling Goose but different proportions -- more compact, more marine-adapted.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: No white cheek patch. White necklace. All-dark head and neck. On saltwater (vs. Canada/Cackling on freshwater or fields).
physical_description.field_id: Small dark goose on saltwater bays, feeding on eelgrass, with no white cheek patch. Distinctive rolling flock murmur. The habitat + appearance combination is diagnostic.

nodes.title: Low Musical Honk Flocks -- E=4 Communal Sound
nodes.description: Rolling throaty 'crronk' at 200-1,200 Hz. Continuous flock murmur on eelgrass beds. More melodious than Canada Goose, softer than Cackling. The bass note of the winter bay soundscape. The most musical goose call in the PNW.

nodes.title: Eelgrass Specialist -- Indicator Species for Bay Health
nodes.description: Feeds almost exclusively on Zostera marina. Head-dipping in shallow water. Most ecologically constrained goose in PNW. Canary in the eelgrass mine. Eelgrass also supports juvenile Chinook salmon, herring, Dungeness crab. Protecting Brant habitat protects the marine food web.

nodes.title: Padilla Bay -- 8,000 Acres of Eelgrass
nodes.description: Primary PNW Brant wintering site. Padilla Bay NERR (National Estuarine Research Reserve). Skagit County, WA. Thousands of Brant at low tide. The defining winter soundscape of the coastal bay ecosystem. Boundary Bay (BC/WA border) is the second major site.

nodes.title: Goose Era Position 3 (129-134) -- The Sea Goose
nodes.description: Canada (129, generalist, E=5), Cackling (130, field, E=4), Brant (131, marine, E=4). Three geese, three habitats, three sounds. Increasing specialization: generalist to field to eelgrass. The sea goose adds the marine dimension.

nodes.title: Fourteenth Consecutive Anatidae (118-131) -- Record Extends Massively
nodes.description: Ducks (118-126), swans (127-128), geese (129-131). Post-climax descent: E=6, E=5, E=4, E=4. First marine specialist since Harlequin (118). Freshwater-to-saltwater gradient complete. The streak spans the full aquatic habitat range.

cross_references.relationship: GOOSE ERA! Cackling (130, E=4, field) and Brant (131, E=4, marine). Both small geese at E=4 but completely different habitats: agricultural field vs. coastal bay eelgrass. Different cheek marks: white patch vs. none.
GOOSE TRIO! Canada (129, E=5, generalist) to Cackling (130, E=4, field) to Brant (131, E=4, marine). Three geese, three habitats, three sounds. Increasing specialization.
STREAK ARC! Harlequin (118, E=5, stream) to Brant (131, E=4, marine). Fourteen consecutive Anatidae. From freshwater torrent to saltwater bay. The full aquatic habitat gradient.
POST-CLIMAX DESCENT! Trumpeter (128, E=6) was the streak's energetic peak. Brant (131, E=4) continues: E=6, E=5, E=4, E=4. The arc approaches symmetry.
EELGRASS CONNECTION! Brant as indicator species for eelgrass health. Eelgrass supports juvenile Chinook salmon, herring, Dungeness crab. Protecting Brant habitat protects the marine food web.
